{"dg-publish":true,"permalink":"/10-19-power/10-power/"}
---

### What is Power?

If we are to address *any* societal issue, let's make sure we're on the same page about a concept fundamental to interpersonal relations: **power**.

In today’s public imagination, one interpretation of power conquers all. A businessman *gains power* as he rises through the ranks in the company. An empire *amasses power* as its territory expands. Super Mario even *powers up* when he obtains a mushroom. This intangible concept is rationalized as a limited resource that accumulates like capital, often at the expense of the "powerless." The more you have, the stronger you are. You can choose your own destiny, and perhaps more importantly, dominate others. *But is it really so simple?*

In the form of money or job description, power is easily measurable and compared. But who has more power between an unassuming billionaire and an armed vigilante? Which is more influential between a coordinated propaganda network and a deeply resonant work of art? And is it possible for even a successful black CEO to be insulated from discrimination or stereotyping?

**All this to say, it's not terribly helpful to *exclusively* consider [[10 - 19 Power/11 Accumulative Forms\|11 Accumulative Forms]] of power.** Power is too context-dependent to be reduced to "more" or "less." It's more like... energy. Aura. Midi-chlorians. [Nanomachines, son](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RhMsboqMMzs). Much like an iceberg, visible expressions of power only represent a small **fraction** of its possible configurations.

Power is complex; it is defined by contradictions. Just as it subjugates, it subtly guides. Just as it is driven through legislation, it is driven through conversation. Just as it is acquired by an economic elite, it is built by people living on the street. In the 21st century, a time of extreme technological change, a complete understanding of power’s nuance is critical.

### History of Power

Though over-theorizing may seem like a fruitless exercise for the immediate needs of addressing social inequity, patching these theories together can illuminate new strategies for change (Eyben et al., 2006).

Robert Dahl, *A has power over B to the extent that A can get B to do something B would not otherwise do*. Actors that hold power intentionally wield it over others.

Peter Bachrach & Morton Baratz introduce a second dimension, where power also operates by controlling who is at the decision-making table. Unlike overt domination, this is hidden from plain view.

Steven Lukes adds the third dimension, where power shapes beliefs, values, and perceptions. It's invisible, because people are likely to not even recognize it is happening.

HAYWARD DISAGREES WITH THIS STUFF, ALL ABOUT BOUNDARIES THAT DEFINE WHAT ACTIONS ARE POSSIBLE. NOT DOMINATION BUT STRUCTURING FIELDS OF ACTION. Power is structural (not possessed) and enables or constrains conditions.

### Uh... Ok What Next

*If you would like to see the full contents of this website, please visit the [[00 - 09 Website/00 Site Index\|00 Site Index]].* 

Power is ubiquitous, though certain [[40 - 49 Power Actors/40 Power Actors/40 Power Actors\|40 Power Actors]] have different relationships with it. 

This wiki is based on a bunch of different theorists and was inspired by PowerCube.net. Read more about some of the [[80 - 89 Power Resources/80 Power Resources/80 Power Resources\|80 Power Resources]] that contributed to the information you see.

This website is primarily a tool to help us identify and unmask power. It is currently a passion project of a busy doctoral student, so expect a lot of missing pieces... if this is helpful, I would eventually like to turn this into a crowdsourced effort, so if you have any suggestions or would like to collaborate, please email belmontindex@gmail.com. I hope you enjoy!
